question: Explain the primary difference between a stack and a queue data structure and provide a real-world application example for each.
expectedPoints: Application example for stack: Undo/Redo functionality in text editors
Application example for queue: Print job scheduling or customer service lines

question: Describe the process of inserting a new node into a Binary Search Tree (BST). What is the time complexity of this operation in the average and worst-case scenarios, and what determines the worst-case performance?
expectedPoints: Start at the root and compare the new node's value with the current node
Traverse left if the value is smaller, right if larger, until a null pointer is found
Insert the new node at that null location
Worst-case time complexity: O(n) for a degenerate/skewed tree
Worst-case is determined by the order of insertion (e.g., inserting sorted data)

question: Compare and contrast the adjacency matrix and adjacency list representations for graphs. Discuss the trade-offs in terms of time complexity for common operations (like checking for an edge, finding all neighbors) and space complexity. Recommend which representation is preferable for a sparse social network graph and justify your choice.
expectedPoints: Adjacency matrix uses O(V²) space, adjacency list uses O(V + E) space
Checking for an edge: O(1) for matrix, O(V) for list in worst-case
Finding all neighbors: O(V) for matrix, O(degree(V)) for list
For a sparse graph (few edges), adjacency list is more space-efficient
Social networks are typically sparse, making the adjacency list preferable due to its lower space complexity and efficient traversal of a node's neighbors
